lesson_objective: A String in Java is an object, not a primitive — and every method that looks like it 'changes' a String actually creates a brand new one.
lesson_summary: This lesson busts the assumption that Strings behave like primitives, using a side-by-side == vs .equals() comparison and a method call whose result is thrown away, to show that Strings are objects and are immutable.
key_points: A String looks like a primitive in code but is actually an object under the hood.
== compares whether two Strings are the same object, not whether they contain the same text.
String methods never modify the original — they return a new String, which you must capture.
step_by_step_explanation: Open with two Strings that look identical, compared with == — seemingly true, then a version built differently that returns false, breaking the assumption.
Introduce .equals() as the fix — it compares actual text content, not identity.
Call .toUpperCase() on a String without reassigning the result, and show the original variable is completely unchanged.
Reassign the result this time and show the transformation actually takes effect.
Land on the takeaway: Strings don't change in place — methods hand you a new one, and it's on you to keep it.

core_example.description: A == comparison that surprises, followed by a .toUpperCase() call whose result is dropped, then correctly captured.
core_example.focus_note: Pause on the line System.out.println(a); right after the ignored toUpperCase() call — that's the moment immutability lands.

analogy: Calling a method on a String is like asking someone to translate a sentence out loud: the original sentence on the page doesn't change — you just get a new spoken version back, and if you don't write it down, it's gone.
common_mistakes: Using == to compare String content instead of .equals().
Assuming a String method like .toUpperCase() modifies the variable it was called on.
what_to_avoid_saying: Do not say == 'sometimes' works for comparing String content and leave it at that — either explain why it can appear to work (string pool), if there's time, or just show the failure case plainly and move on.
Do not say Strings 'can't be changed at all' without qualifying that a variable can still be reassigned to a new String — it's the existing String object that never changes.
beginner_takeaway: A String method's result isn't optional to capture — no assignment means nothing changed.
retention_hook: Two Strings that look exactly the same. One line of code says they're not equal. Here's why Java isn't wrong.
visual_notes: Show both String declarations side by side with a visual 'are these the same object?' question mark before revealing the == result.
Show the ignored toUpperCase() line with a 'nothing happened' stamp before showing the corrected, reassigned version.

flagged_uncertainties: Whether to explain the string constant pool (why two identical literals often are == equal) was left out for time; flagged in case a future revision has room to address it without overcomplicating the core point.
